lessons.title: Work: Force Times Distance
lessons.content:
When you push a shopping cart across a parking lot, you can *feel* that you have done something tiring. Physics has a precise name for that something: **work**. But the physics meaning is narrower than the everyday one, and that surprises a lot of people.

**Build:** In physics, **work** means using a force to move something through a distance. A **force** is just a push or a pull, measured in a unit called the **newton (N)**. If a force of size $F$ pushes an object a distance $d$ in the direction of the push, the work done is force times distance:

$$W = F \times d$$

We measure work in a unit called the **joule (J)**. One joule is the work done by a 1-newton push over 1 meter. So pushing with 10 N over 3 m does $10 \times 3 = 30$ J of work.

Here is the surprising part. If nothing moves, **no work is done** in the physics sense, no matter how hard you strain. Holding a heavy box perfectly still is exhausting, but because the box does not move ($d = 0$), the physics work is zero. Also, if the force points *sideways* to the motion (perpendicular to it), that force does no work either, because it is not pushing the object along its path.

**Recap:** Work means moving something with a force: $W = F \times d$, measured in joules. No movement (or a sideways force) means no work, even when it feels hard.

items.prompt: In physics, what does it mean to do "work"?
choices: Using a force to move something through a distance
Feeling tired after an effort
Holding a heavy object steady
Thinking hard about a problem
items.explanation: Work in physics is precisely force acting over a distance moved. Feeling tired or holding something still can be exhausting, but if nothing moves, no physics work is done.

items.prompt: What is the formula for work done by a force in the direction of motion?
choices: W = F x d (force times distance)
W = F + d (force plus distance)
W = F / d (force divided by distance)
W = d / F (distance divided by force)
items.explanation: Work is force multiplied by the distance moved: W = F x d. Adding or dividing them does not match how a steady push over a longer distance does more work.

items.prompt: What is the unit of work?
choices: The joule (J)
The newton (N)
The meter (m)
The watt (W)
items.explanation: Work is measured in joules (J). The newton measures force and the meter measures distance; multiply them and you get joules. The watt is a different quantity (power).

items.prompt: You push a box with a steady force of 20 N and it slides 4 m in the direction you push. How much work did you do?
choices: 5 J
items.explanation: Work = force x distance = 20 N x 4 m = 80 J. The distractor 24 comes from adding (20 + 4), and 5 comes from dividing (20 / 4); neither is how work is calculated.

items.prompt: You hold a heavy suitcase perfectly still at your side for two minutes. How much physics work do you do on the suitcase?
choices: Zero, because it does not move
A lot, because it is heavy
It depends on the suitcase's weight
Exactly its weight in joules
items.explanation: Work needs movement: W = F x d, and here d = 0, so the work is zero no matter how heavy it is. It feels tiring because your muscles burn energy holding tension, but that is not work done on the suitcase.

items.prompt: A waiter carries a tray straight across a level floor at steady speed. The force holding the tray up points straight up; the tray moves horizontally. How much work does that upward force do on the tray?
choices: Zero, because the force is perpendicular to the motion
A large amount, since the tray travels far
It equals the weight times the distance walked
It is negative because walking is hard
items.explanation: A force does work only along the direction of motion. The upward holding force is at right angles (perpendicular) to the horizontal travel, so it does no work. This is the classic perpendicular-force case.

items.prompt: Two people each push identical boxes with the same 50 N force. Box A slides 2 m; box B slides 6 m. Who does more work?
choices: The person pushing box B, because it moved farther
The person pushing box A, because it was harder
They do equal work, since the force is the same
Neither does work, since the force is the same
items.explanation: With the same force, work depends on distance: W = F x d. Box B moved farther (6 m), so 50 x 6 = 300 J beats 50 x 2 = 100 J. Equal force does not mean equal work when distances differ.

items.prompt: One joule is best described as the work done when...
choices: a force of 1 newton moves something 1 meter
a mass of 1 kilogram is lifted any height
1 second of effort passes
a force of 1 newton is applied without moving anything
items.explanation: A joule is 1 newton x 1 meter. The other options miss the definition: lifting depends on the actual height, time alone is not work, and a force with no movement does zero work.

items.prompt: Why can pushing hard against a locked, immovable wall be tiring yet involve zero physics work?
choices: The wall does not move, so the distance is zero
Walls cannot have forces applied to them
Work is only about how tired you feel
The force you apply is too small to count
items.explanation: Work = force x distance. The wall stays put, so d = 0 and the work is zero. Your muscles still burn energy maintaining the push, which is why it tires you, but no work is done on the wall.

lessons.title: Kinetic and Potential Energy
lessons.content:
Why does a moving car cause damage in a crash, while the same car parked does nothing? And why does a rock held high above your foot feel threatening? The answer is **energy** - the capacity to do work. Two everyday kinds matter most here.

**Build:** **Kinetic energy** is the energy of *motion*. Anything moving has it: a thrown ball, a rolling cart, flowing water. The faster and heavier the object, the more kinetic energy it carries. The formula is:

$$KE = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$$

Here $m$ is the **mass** (how much stuff is in the object, in kilograms) and $v$ is the **speed** (in meters per second). Notice the speed is *squared*: doubling the speed gives four times the kinetic energy. That is why high speed is so dangerous.

**Potential energy** is *stored* energy waiting to be released, because of an object's position. The most common type is **gravitational potential energy** - energy stored by lifting something up high:

$$PE = m \times g \times h$$

where $h$ is the **height** above the ground and $g$ is the **gravitational field strength** near Earth, about 9.8 newtons per kilogram (which tells you how strongly gravity pulls). Lift a book onto a shelf and you store PE in it; let it fall and that PE turns into KE.

**Recap:** Kinetic energy is energy of motion ($KE = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$, and speed counts double because it is squared). Potential energy is stored energy of position, like a lifted object ($PE = mgh$). Both are measured in joules.

items.prompt: What is kinetic energy?
choices: The energy an object has because it is moving
The energy stored by lifting something high
The energy released only by burning fuel
A force that pushes moving objects
items.explanation: Kinetic energy is the energy of motion. Stored energy from height is potential energy; energy is not a force, and fuel is only one of many sources.

items.prompt: Which formula gives kinetic energy?
choices: KE = 1/2 x m x v^2
KE = m x g x h
KE = m x v
KE = F x d
items.explanation: Kinetic energy is one-half mass times speed squared. The m x g x h formula is gravitational potential energy, m x v is momentum, and F x d is work.

items.prompt: A car doubles its speed (mass unchanged). What happens to its kinetic energy?
choices: It becomes four times as large
It doubles
It stays the same
It becomes half as large
items.explanation: Speed is squared in KE = 1/2 m v^2, so doubling v multiplies KE by 2^2 = 4. People often expect it to merely double; the squaring is exactly why high speed is so much more dangerous.

items.prompt: What is gravitational potential energy?
choices: Stored energy an object has because of its height above the ground
The energy of an object only while it is falling
The total weight of an object
Energy that exists only inside batteries
items.explanation: Gravitational PE is energy stored by an object's raised position, ready to be released if it falls. While falling it is converting to kinetic energy; weight is a force, not energy.

items.prompt: Which formula gives gravitational potential energy?
choices: PE = m x g x h
PE = 1/2 x m x v^2
PE = m x v
PE = g / h
items.explanation: Gravitational PE is mass times g (gravitational field strength, about 9.8 N/kg) times height. The 1/2 m v^2 form is kinetic energy.

items.prompt: Lift a 2 kg book to a height of 3 m. Using g = 9.8 N/kg, what is its gravitational potential energy?
choices: About 59 J
About 6 J
About 30 J
About 5 J
items.explanation: PE = m x g x h = 2 x 9.8 x 3 = 58.8 J, about 59 J. The 6 comes from ignoring g (2 x 3), and 5 from m + h; the formula multiplies all three.

items.prompt: A roller-coaster cart sits still at the very top of a tall hill. Compared with the bottom, where is its energy mostly found?
choices: Mostly potential energy, since it is high but not moving
Mostly kinetic energy, since it is on a coaster
Equal kinetic and potential energy
It has no energy until it starts moving
items.explanation: At the top it is high (large PE) but barely moving (tiny KE). As it descends, that stored PE converts into KE, so it speeds up at the bottom. It clearly has energy even while paused at the top.

items.prompt: Two balls fall from the same shelf. Ball A has twice the mass of ball B. At the same speed, how do their kinetic energies compare?
choices: Ball A has twice the kinetic energy
They are equal, since speed decides KE
Ball A has four times the kinetic energy
Ball B has more, since lighter things move easier
items.explanation: In KE = 1/2 m v^2 mass appears once (not squared), so doubling the mass at the same speed doubles the KE. Only speed is squared; mass scales KE directly.

items.prompt: What unit is energy (both kinetic and potential) measured in?
choices: The joule (J)
The newton (N)
The meter per second (m/s)
The kilogram (kg)
items.explanation: All forms of energy, including kinetic and potential, are measured in joules - the same unit as work. Newtons measure force, m/s measures speed, and kilograms measure mass.

lessons.title: Conservation of Energy
lessons.content:
Watch a pendulum swing or a ball roll down a ramp and you will notice energy seems to flow from one form to another, yet nothing simply vanishes. This is one of the deepest rules in all of physics: the **conservation of energy**.

**Build:** The law of **conservation of energy** says that energy can never be created or destroyed - it only **transforms** from one form into another. The total amount of energy in a closed system stays the same. Think of energy like money you move between pockets: the form changes, but the total is unchanged.

Drop a ball from a shelf. At the top it is all **potential energy** (stored energy of height). As it falls, that PE smoothly converts into **kinetic energy** (energy of motion), so it speeds up. At any moment, $PE + KE$ stays constant if nothing else interferes.

But where does motion go when a real ball finally stops bouncing? This is the big beginner trap: energy is *not* "used up". Instead, **friction** - the rubbing force between surfaces - converts the motion energy into **heat** (and a little sound). The energy is still there; it just spread out as warmth into the ball, floor, and air. That is why brakes get hot and rubbed hands feel warm.

**Recap:** Energy is never lost, only changed in form, and the total stays constant. A falling object trades PE for KE; friction turns motion energy into heat. "Disappeared" energy has really just transformed.

items.prompt: What does the law of conservation of energy state?
choices: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
Energy slowly disappears over time
Energy is created whenever something moves
Energy only exists in moving objects
items.explanation: Conservation of energy means the total amount never changes; energy only shifts between forms. It is not created by motion nor destroyed over time.

items.prompt: A ball is dropped from a shelf. As it falls, which energy change happens?
choices: Potential energy converts into kinetic energy
Kinetic energy converts into potential energy
Both energies disappear
Energy is created as it speeds up
items.explanation: Falling means losing height (less PE) and gaining speed (more KE), so PE turns into KE. The reverse happens when something rises; energy is neither created nor destroyed in the swap.

items.prompt: A sliding box on a rough floor gradually stops. What happened to its kinetic energy?
choices: Friction converted it into heat
It was destroyed completely
It turned back into the floor's weight
It simply ran out, like fuel
items.explanation: Energy is never destroyed. Friction transforms the box's motion energy into heat (and a little sound), warming the box and floor. "Ran out" wrongly suggests energy can vanish.

items.prompt: At the very bottom of a swing, a pendulum is moving fastest. Where is most of its energy?
choices: In kinetic energy, since it is moving fast and is low
In potential energy, since it swings high elsewhere
Split exactly half and half at every point
It has lost most of its energy by the bottom
items.explanation: At the lowest point the height is least (small PE) and speed is greatest (large KE), so energy is mostly kinetic. It trades back to PE as it rises on the other side; the total stays the same.

items.prompt: Why do a car's brakes get hot when it stops?
choices: The car's kinetic energy is converted into heat by friction
Heat is created from nothing by the wheels
The car's potential energy is destroyed
The engine pushes heat into the brakes
items.explanation: Braking uses friction to transform the car's motion energy into heat in the brake parts. The energy is conserved, just changed in form; it is not created from nothing or destroyed.

items.prompt: Ignoring friction, a cart starts at the top of a hill with 500 J of potential energy and rolls down. What is its kinetic energy at the bottom?
choices: About 500 J
About 250 J
About 0 J
About 1000 J
items.explanation: With no friction, all the PE converts to KE, so KE at the bottom equals the 500 J it started with. Half (250) or double (1000) would violate conservation of energy.

items.prompt: A common beginner belief is that energy gets "used up" and disappears. Why is this wrong?
choices: Energy only changes form; the total is always conserved
Energy really does vanish, the belief is correct
Energy is used up but new energy is created to replace it
Only kinetic energy can be used up
items.explanation: Energy is conserved: when it seems "used up", it has actually spread out into other forms like heat. Nothing vanishes, and no new energy appears to replace it.

items.prompt: In a real roller coaster (with friction), the cart cannot quite reach the same height on the next hill. Why?
choices: Some energy turned into heat through friction, so less is left to climb
Energy was destroyed by the second hill
Gravity got stronger on the way up
The cart created extra potential energy
items.explanation: Friction continually converts some motion energy into heat, so less mechanical energy remains to lift the cart, and it reaches a lower height. The total energy (including the heat) is still conserved.

items.prompt: Energy transforms but the total stays the same. Which everyday example shows this?
choices: A toaster turning electrical energy into heat and light
A rock gaining brand-new energy by sitting still
A battery destroying energy as it powers a phone
A fan creating energy from nothing
items.explanation: A toaster converts electrical energy into heat and light, a clear transformation with the total conserved. Energy cannot be created from nothing (fan, sitting rock) or destroyed (battery).

lessons.title: Power and Efficiency
lessons.content:
Two movers carry the same load up the same stairs. One does it in 10 seconds, the other takes a full minute. They did the *same work*, so what is different? The answer is **power** - how *fast* the energy is delivered.

**Build:** **Power** is the rate of using energy, or doing work: how much energy per unit of time.

$$P = \frac{E}{t}$$

Here $E$ is the energy used (in joules) and $t$ is the time taken (in seconds). Power is measured in **watts (W)**, where 1 watt means 1 joule of energy per second. A 60-watt light bulb uses 60 joules every second. The key idea: doing the *same* job in *less time* needs *more power*. A car engine rated higher in watts can speed up faster, even if a slower engine could eventually do the same work.

Do not confuse the two: **energy** is the total amount of "fuel" used, while **power** is how quickly you spend it.

Real machines are never perfect. **Efficiency** tells you how much of the energy you put in actually does the useful job, instead of being lost as heat or sound:

$$\text{efficiency} = \frac{\text{useful energy out}}{\text{total energy in}}$$

If a motor takes in 100 J and delivers 80 J of useful work, it is 80% efficient; the other 20 J became waste heat. Efficiency is always less than 100% because some energy always escapes.

**Recap:** Power is energy per time, measured in watts ($P = E / t$); same work done faster means more power. Efficiency is useful out over total in, and is always below 100% in real machines.

items.prompt: What is power in physics?
choices: The rate at which energy is used or work is done
The total amount of energy stored
The force needed to move something
The height an object is lifted to
items.explanation: Power is how fast energy is used - energy per unit time. Total stored energy is a different quantity (just energy), and force and height are not power.

items.prompt: Which formula gives power?
choices: P = E / t (energy divided by time)
P = E x t (energy times time)
P = E + t (energy plus time)
P = t / E (time divided by energy)
items.explanation: Power is energy divided by the time taken. Multiplying or adding time would not capture the idea of a rate; flipping it to t/E gives the wrong quantity.

items.prompt: What is the unit of power?
choices: The watt (W)
The joule (J)
The newton (N)
The second (s)
items.explanation: Power is measured in watts, where 1 watt is 1 joule per second. The joule measures energy, the newton measures force, and the second measures time.

items.prompt: A machine uses 600 J of energy in 3 seconds. What is its power?
items.explanation: Power = energy / time = 600 / 3 = 200 W. The 1800 comes from multiplying, and 603 from adding; power is a rate, so you divide energy by time.

items.prompt: Two workers do the same 1000 J of work. Worker A finishes in 5 s, worker B in 20 s. Who has more power?
choices: Worker A, because the same work was done in less time
Worker B, because slower means steadier
They have equal power, since the work is equal
Neither, because work and power are the same thing
items.explanation: Same work in less time means more power: A delivers 1000/5 = 200 W versus B's 1000/20 = 50 W. Equal work does not mean equal power when the times differ; work and power are not the same thing.

items.prompt: What does "efficiency" of a machine mean?
choices: The useful energy out divided by the total energy in
The total energy a machine can store
The speed at which a machine runs
The total energy in divided by the useful energy out
items.explanation: Efficiency is useful energy out over total energy in - the fraction of input that does the useful job. Flipping the ratio gives a number above 1, which is not how efficiency is defined.

items.prompt: A motor takes in 200 J and delivers 150 J of useful work. What is its efficiency?
items.explanation: Efficiency = useful out / total in = 150 / 200 = 0.75 = 75%. The 50 J is just the waste heat (not an efficiency), and 133% comes from dividing the wrong way - real efficiency can never exceed 100%.

items.prompt: Why is a real machine's efficiency always less than 100%?
choices: Some input energy always escapes as waste heat or sound
Machines create extra energy they cannot use
Efficiency is just chosen by the manufacturer
Energy is destroyed inside every machine
items.explanation: Some energy always leaks away as heat, sound, or friction losses, so the useful output is always less than the input. Energy is not destroyed (it is conserved) or created; it just spreads into non-useful forms.

items.prompt: Which statement correctly separates energy from power?
choices: Energy is the total amount used; power is how fast it is used
Energy and power mean exactly the same thing
Power is the total amount used; energy is how fast it is used
Energy is measured in watts; power in joules
items.explanation: Energy (joules) is the total amount; power (watts) is the rate of using it. The roles and units must not be swapped - joules for energy, watts for power.
